title: SEGMENT 16 — The honest discussion — what worked, what didn't, what we don't know

lines.text: SEGMENT 16 — The honest discussion — what worked, what didn't, what we don't know
Let me synthesize, in this segment, the picture we have at the end of the paper.
Because I want you to be able to articulate, in your own words, an honest summary of what milasen did and did not do.
What worked.
The molecular diagnosis was achieved by whole-genome sequencing plus careful manual inspection of chimeric reads, identifying a previously undescribed SVA insertion in MFSD8 intron 6.
The molecular mechanism — cryptic splice acceptor activation leading to a premature stop codon — was characterized in detail.
A splice-modulating ASO was designed against the cryptic acceptor and validated in patient-derived fibroblasts, where it tripled correct splicing and rescued lysosomal phenotypes.
An abbreviated preclinical safety program in rats, leaning heavily on platform safety data from nusinersen, established a tolerable dose range.
The FDA authorized administration under Expanded Access.
The drug was manufactured, dosed in escalating fashion intrathecally, and tolerated without serious adverse events.
Seizures fell substantially.
Other clinical measures stabilized.
What didn't work, or didn't work fully.
The underlying brain atrophy continued.
The drug slowed but did not halt or reverse the neurodegeneration.
Mila's eventual clinical decline continued, more slowly than projected from her pre-treatment trajectory, but not stopped.
What we don't know, and could not know, from this case.
Whether starting treatment earlier — at age two or three, before substantial brain damage — would have produced a much better outcome.
Whether a higher dose of milasen would have produced more complete splicing correction with acceptable safety.
Whether milasen would have eventually been more effective if continued for many years rather than the one year reported in the paper.

Whether the residual mis-splicing — the sixty-six percent of transcripts that were still being incorrectly spliced even on treatment — was the limiting factor or whether other aspects of the disease biology were also at play.
None of these questions can be answered from a single patient.

They could only be answered by treating additional CLN7 patients with milasen, which has not, to my knowledge, been pursued at scale, because the SVA insertion that drives Mila's disease is itself unique to her family — no other reported CLN7 patient has the same molecular defect, so the same drug would not be expected to help them.
This is the deepest difficulty with individualized therapy.
Every patient is, by definition, the only data point.
There is no clinical trial.
There is no statistical analysis.
There is no comparison group.

There is just one patient, one drug, one set of observations, and a lot of uncertainty about whether the observed effects were truly drug-related or simply within the range of natural variation in this particular disease.
The paper's authors handle this difficulty with care.
They report the seizure results, which are the most quantitative and the most clearly distinguishable from baseline noise.
They report the other clinical measures honestly.
They report the MRI findings without trying to spin them.
They acknowledge the limitations of an N-of-1 study.
They make a defensible scientific case that milasen had a real clinical effect on seizures, without overclaiming about disease modification.

For the reader of the paper, the appropriate stance is to accept the seizure data as strong evidence that the drug worked at the level of its proximate molecular and clinical targets, to accept the safety data as preliminary but encouraging, to accept the MRI data as a sobering reminder that the underlying pathology was not reversed, and to hold the entire case as a proof of concept for the approach rather than as definitive evidence of efficacy for this particular drug.
There is one more piece of the honest discussion I want to flag, because it has become a significant issue in the field.
Individualized therapies do not, in general, have a clear path to becoming approved drugs.
Milasen will never be approved by the FDA as a marketable drug, because the patient population is one.
There is no clinical trial that could be run.
There is no regulatory pathway by which milasen could move from compassionate use in Mila to broader availability.
The drug exists only for her, and now that she is gone, the drug exists only as a manufactured substance with no intended use.
That has implications for how we think about what we are doing when we develop these therapies.
We are not, in the conventional sense, developing drugs.

We are doing something more like custom molecular medicine — designing a specific molecule for a specific patient, manufacturing it for that patient, and dosing it to that patient under regulatory authorization that applies only to that case.

The framework looks more like surgery, in a way, or like a custom prosthetic — a one-off intervention crafted for one person — than like the population-level drug development the pharmaceutical industry was built around.

And the institutions, the funding mechanisms, the regulatory frameworks, the ethical structures, and the public expectations of what a "drug" is have all been built for the population model and have to be rebuilt, or at least supplemented, to handle the individualized model.
That rebuilding has begun.
Which brings us to what happened after the paper was published.
